| [Fly-half](/wiki/Fly-half_(rugby_union) "Fly-half (rugby union)"), [Centre](/wiki/Centre_(rugby_union) "Centre (rugby union)") | | | | **Current team** | [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Waikato (National Provincial Championship)") | | | | Senior career | | | | | **Years** | **Team** | **Apps** | **(Points)** | | 2010–2013 | [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Wellington (National Provincial Championship)") | 37 | (370) | | 2011–2018 | [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby_union) "Highlanders (rugby union)") | 88 | (853) | | 2014–2017 | [Southland](/wiki/Southland_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Southland (National Provincial Championship)") | 17 | (187) | | 2018–2021 | [Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") | 53 | (287) | | 2021–2023 | [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon_OU_Rugby "Lyon OU Rugby") | 29 | (147) | | 2023–2025 | [Shimizu Blue Sharks](/wiki/Shimizu_Koto_Blue_Sharks "Shimizu Koto Blue Sharks") | 21 | (77) | | 2025– | [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Waikato (National Provincial Championship)") | 6 | (28) | | Correct as of 28 July 2025 | | | | | International career | | | | | **Years** | **Team** | **Apps** | **(Points)** | | 2011 | [New Zealand U20](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_under-20_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national under-20 rugby union team") | 4 | (15) | | 2015–2017 | [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") | 16 | (55) | | 2023 | [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_union_team "Samoa national rugby union team") | 4 | (27) | | Correct as of 28 July 2025 | | | | | |

(born 3 February 1991) is a professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") player who plays as a [fly-half](/wiki/Fly-half_(rugby_union) "Fly-half (rugby union)") for [Japan Rugby League One](/wiki/Japan_Rugby_League_One "Japan Rugby League One") club [Shimizu Blue Sharks](/wiki/Shimizu_Koto_Blue_Sharks "Shimizu Koto Blue Sharks"), and for [Bunnings NPC](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(2006-present) "National Provincial Championship (2006-present)") club [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Waikato (National Provincial Championship)"). Born in New Zealand, he represents [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_union_team "Samoa national rugby union team") at international level after qualifying on [ancestry grounds](/wiki/International_rugby_union_eligibility_rules "International rugby union eligibility rules").

**Lima Zachariah Sopoaga** (born 3 February 1991) is a professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") player who plays as a [fly-half](/wiki/Fly-half_(rugby_union) "Fly-half (rugby union)") for [Japan Rugby League One](/wiki/Japan_Rugby_League_One "Japan Rugby League One") club [Shimizu Blue Sharks](/wiki/Shimizu_Koto_Blue_Sharks "Shimizu Koto Blue Sharks"), and for [Bunnings NPC](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(2006-present) "National Provincial Championship (2006-present)") club [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(National_Provincial_Championship) "Waikato (National Provincial Championship)"). Born in New Zealand, he represents [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_union_team "Samoa national rugby union team") at international level after qualifying on [ancestry grounds](/wiki/International_rugby_union_eligibility_rules "International rugby union eligibility rules").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Sopoaga was born in Wellington, New Zealand. His family comes from Samoa and the Cook Islands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sopoaga is the oldest among six siblings. His younger brother, [Tupou Sopoaga](/wiki/Tupou_Sopoaga "Tupou Sopoaga"), played for the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") in the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") but switched codes and played alongside Lima at the Highlanders for a time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In his high school days, Lima Sopoaga attended [Wellington College](/wiki/Wellington_College_(New_Zealand) "Wellington College (New Zealand)") and won four local championships with the 1st XV.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He captained the school's side in 2009 and was selected for the NZ Secondary Schools team.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Club career")]

### Wellington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wellington")]

At the age of 19, Sopoaga played for [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington_Rugby_Union "Wellington Rugby Union") in the [2010 ITM Cup](/wiki/2010_ITM_Cup "2010 ITM Cup"), making his debut against [Manawatu](/wiki/Manawatu_Rugby_Union "Manawatu Rugby Union").[[1]](#cite_note-pr-1) His strong performances established himself as the squad's starting number 10. He finished the season with 88 points in 9 appearances to help Wellington to reach the final of the competition, where he scored 21 points despite losing to [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Rugby_Union "Canterbury Rugby Union").

### Southland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Southland")]

In late May 2014, Sopoaga announced that he will be joining the [Southland Stags](/wiki/Southland_Stags "Southland Stags") for the 2014 and 2015 seasons. The transfer from the [Wellington Lions](/wiki/Wellington_Lions "Wellington Lions") to the Stags keeps Sopoaga in [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby_union) "Highlanders (rugby union)") territory. Playing for Southland, Lima joins another current Highlander in [Elliot Dixon](/wiki/Elliot_Dixon "Elliot Dixon").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sopoaga, excited by the move as reported by Rugby Southland; "The opportunity came up and it is a good fit to stay in the Highlander franchise and represent the region in the Super 15 and ITM Cup. I’m really starting to feel at home in the south now and proud to be part of the region. The Stags boys in the Highlanders have nothing but good things to say about the Stags and the Southland province and I’m looking forward to getting to Southland and giving my all for my new team."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Highlanders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Highlanders")]

Sopoaga followed his former Wellington coach [Jamie Joseph](/wiki/Jamie_Joseph "Jamie Joseph") south, signing on with the [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby) "Highlanders (rugby)") for the [2011 Super Rugby season](/wiki/2011_Super_Rugby_season "2011 Super Rugby season"). Expected to back up [Colin Slade](/wiki/Colin_Slade "Colin Slade"), he ended up starting the opening game of the season against the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)")[[1]](#cite_note-pr-1) due to Slade being injured. He responded in fine fashion by scoring a try and kicking two penalties in a 14–9 victory. However, in his second start of the season he suffered a shoulder injury which ruled him out of the team until the late stages of the season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2015 he helped guide the Highlanders to their first super rugby title by defeating the 1st place Hurricanes 21–14. Sopoaga scored the most points of any Super Rugby player in 2015, scoring 191 points that season.

The Highlanders failed to make the Grand Final in 2016, losing 42–30 against the [Lions](/wiki/Lions_(Super_Rugby) "Lions (Super Rugby)") in the semi-final. Sopoaga was one of the few Highlanders to shine in the 2016 semi-final, scoring a try and kicking accurately, ending his [2016 Super Rugby season](/wiki/2016_Super_Rugby_season "2016 Super Rugby season") with 186 points.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sopoaga was pulled from the field in a 16–12 win over the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") on 11 March 2017 after suffering a hamstring injury when he performed a try-saving tackle on All Blacks team-mate and Blues midfielder [George Moala](/wiki/George_Moala "George Moala"). Sopoaga made his return from injury to replace [Marty Banks](/wiki/Marty_Banks "Marty Banks") off the bench in a 55–6 win over the [Western Force](/wiki/Western_Force "Western Force") on 20 May 2017. The departing Banks scored more points in the [2017 Super Rugby season](/wiki/2017_Super_Rugby_season "2017 Super Rugby season") than Sopoaga due to Sopoaga spending two months out injured. Sopoaga scored 54 points in the 2017 season, failing to score any in the quarter-final against the [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusaders_(rugby_union) "Crusaders (rugby union)") where the Highlanders lost 17–0.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sopoaga started for the Highlanders against the touring [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") side on 13 June 2017, contributing eight points to the Highlanders' narrow 23–22 win over the Lions before being replaced by Banks in the 54th minute.

### Wasps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Wasps")]

On 15 January 2018, Sopoaga confirmed to be leaving the Highlanders and New Zealand to join top English side [Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") in the [Gallagher Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") from the 2018–19 season.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Sopoaga made his Wasps debut on 8 September 2018 in a 31–42 defeat to [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs"), he came on as a substitute in the second half and scored a conversion from a try scored by [Dan Robson](/wiki/Dan_Robson "Dan Robson").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He made his full starting debut the following game week, on 16 September, scoring 18 points (three conversions and four penalties) in a high scoring, 41–35 win over the [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Lyon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lyon")]

On 13 April 2021, Sopoaga would leave Wasps as he will travel to France to join [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14") outfit [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon_OU "Lyon OU") from the 2021-22 season.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International career")]

After an injury to Chiefs captain [Aaron Cruden](/wiki/Aaron_Cruden "Aaron Cruden"), Sopoaga's Super Rugby efforts in 2015 saw him gaining selection for New Zealand.[[1]](#cite_note-pr-1) Sopoaga was named in the [All Blacks](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team")' 41-man squad for the [2015 Rugby Championship](/wiki/2015_Rugby_Championship "2015 Rugby Championship"), making his international debut against South Africa in Johannesburg on 25 July 2015, debuting alongside lock [James Broadhurst](/wiki/James_Broadhurst "James Broadhurst"). Sopoaga started the match and was not subbed off, with replacement [Beauden Barrett](/wiki/Beauden_Barrett "Beauden Barrett") replacing [Israel Dagg](/wiki/Israel_Dagg "Israel Dagg") off the bench instead of Sopoaga who scored 12 points on debut, guiding the All Blacks to a 27–20 win. Sopoaga narrowly missed out on selection for the [2015 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2015_Rugby_World_Cup "2015 Rugby World Cup"), with selectors preferring 100-plus test veteran [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter") and the versatility of [Colin Slade](/wiki/Colin_Slade "Colin Slade") and Barrett.

Sopoaga was re-selected for the All Blacks in the Steinlager series against Wales in 2016. After Cruden was injured again, Sopoaga made his second test appearance in the third test against Wales on 25 June 2016 which was a 46–6 win at Sopoaga'a home crowd in Dunedin. Sopoaga replaced [Ryan Crotty](/wiki/Ryan_Crotty "Ryan Crotty") off the bench in the 55th minute, covering inside centre for the rest of the test. Sopoaga made four more appearances in the black jersey in 2016, scoring 10 points that year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the departure of Aaron Cruden to France and the inexperienced [Damian McKenzie](/wiki/Damian_McKenzie "Damian McKenzie") being used at fullback, Sopoaga carried a far heavier workload for the All Blacks in the 2017 season. Sopoaga replaced Beauden Barrett in the 59th minute of a 78–0 win over Samoa, converting two tries. Sopoaga also went across for what would have been his first try that match, but it was disallowed after he received a forward pass from replacement scrum-half [TJ Perenara](/wiki/TJ_Perenara "TJ Perenara"). Following the win over Samoa, Sopoaga made another 11 appearances for the All Blacks that year. Sopoaga scored his first test try on 16 September 2017 in a 57-0 demolishing of South Africa after replacing man-of-the match [Nehe Milner-Skudder](/wiki/Nehe_Milner-Skudder "Nehe Milner-Skudder") in the 53rd minute.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Concussion to Barrett saw Sopoaga step up in the second test against South Africa in 2017, replacing Barrett only 33 minutes into the game. Sopoaga scored 7 points that day, converting tries by McKenzie and winger [Rieko Ioane](/wiki/Rieko_Ioane "Rieko Ioane"). Sopoaga also attempted a drop goal in the 76th minute but missed. Springbok replacement back [Damian de Allende](/wiki/Damian_de_Allende "Damian de Allende") attempted to charge down the kick but was controversially red-carded for a late hit on Sopoaga. The Springboks were further punished by De Allende's mistake because of Sopoaga turning the penalty given into 3 points, the deciding factor in the All Blacks' 25–24 win that day.

Barrett's continued concussion symptoms saw Sopoaga named to start for only the second time in his international career, starting against the Wallabies for 2017's third Bledisloe Cup test on 22 October 2017. An attempted pass from Sopoaga to midfielder [Sonny Bill Williams](/wiki/Sonny_Bill_Williams "Sonny Bill Williams") went wrong in the 6th minute of the test, with Wallabies winger [Reece Hodge](/wiki/Reece_Hodge "Reece Hodge") intercepting to score a try. Sopoaga recovered well to score 8 points but was subbed off in the 60th minute for [David Havili](/wiki/David_Havili "David Havili"), with McKenzie moving into first-five. The Wallabies went on to win 23–18.

Sopoaga made an appearance in every All Blacks fixture on the 2017 end-of-season tour, replacing Havili and McKenzie against the Barbarians and France. Sopoaga earned a mid-week start against a French XV on 14 November 2017 where Sopoaga did not miss a kick at goal. He was replaced by debutant [Richie Mo'unga](/wiki/Richie_Mo%27unga "Richie Mo'unga") in the 53rd minute. Sopoaga followed up the performance against the French XV by replacing [Waisake Naholo](/wiki/Waisake_Naholo "Waisake Naholo") off the bench against Scotland and Wales. Sopoaga's signing announced with the Wasps will likely mean his appearance off the bench against Wales on 25 November 2017 will be his last test for New Zealand.

In late 2023, Sopoaga was selected to play for [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_union_team "Samoa national rugby union team") for the [2023 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2023_Rugby_World_Cup "2023 Rugby World Cup") in France. He made his first appearance in a 28 to 14 victory over the [Barbarians](/wiki/Barbarian_F.C. "Barbarian F.C."). He earned his first official cap the following week in a close 13 to 17 loss to [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team"). During pool play at the World Cup, Sopoaga appeared in two matches: the first was off of the bench in a dominant win over [Chile](/wiki/Chile_national_rugby_union_team "Chile national rugby union team"), while the second match saw him start at fly-half in a one point loss to [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Sopoaga missed the second test against Argentina in 2017 due to the birth of his first child, a daughter named Milla.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lima_Sopoaga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: References")]
